artifacts.content:
Hi Daniel,

Just finished my tea and figured I’d write before I get sucked into another “one more chapter” of this mystery novel.

I’ve been thinking a lot about how we’re both juggling everyone else’s needs right now. Between my clients at Foothills, the seniors I see, and family stuff, my brain feels like it’s on permanent “on-call.” Since March 2023 I’ve been seeing a counsellor at Calgary Counselling Centre – her name’s Dr. Melissa Carter – about twice a month. We’ve been working through the grief from Bà Ngoại’s years in long-term care and how it still hits me out of nowhere. My last session was literally titled “Processing long-term care losses – May 12, 2024” in her portal, which felt weirdly on-the-nose.

I told her about that October 2021 mess at Silver Meadows in Edmonton, when we found Bà in Room 214 in soiled clothes and ended up sending that big complaint email (“Concerns about dignity and care for resident Huong Le – Room 214”) to the unit manager and Alberta Health Services Patient Relations. She keeps saying it makes sense that I’m so intense about dignity and routine with my own clients now.

Physically I’m trying to be smarter too. My back and right shoulder have been grumpy since late 2022 from all the transfers at Foothills. Remember when I went to Southland Physiotherapy & Wellness Centre in January? Mark Jensen, the physio there, wrote “early signs of overuse injury in OTA duties” in his report and told me to cut back on heavy lifts. So I’m doing my stretches like a good nerd and being more assertive about asking for help with bigger clients.

Money-wise, I’m still sending Aunt Kim $250–$300 a month by e-Transfer to chip away at that TD Canada Trust line of credit for Bà’s long-term care and funeral. Dad keeps saying we’re almost there, but it still means saying no to random trips and fancy dinners. It’s okay, though – Glenmore Reservoir walks and home-cooked bò kho beat Vegas anyway.

How are things on your end? How’s “Vancouver roommate stuff” going? (Yes, I’m using the code phrase.) I’m proud of you for figuring things out at your own pace. Whenever you’re ready to talk to Mom and Dad, I’m on your team – but there’s no rush.

Call me this weekend? Maybe Sunday evening after Mom’s done feeding us.

Love,
Amy

Hi Jason,

Just got home from work and my feet are finally up, so I wanted to write this out while it’s still clear in my head instead of half-asleep mumbling on the phone.

I’ve been circling around the whole “moving in together” topic again, especially since it’s been almost a year since that huge argument at your condo near Chinook LRT last November. I still remember standing in your kitchen, both of us exhausted, and you blurting out that you feel pressured to support your parents financially and aren’t ready to commit to a joint lease. I went home that night and poured everything into a journal entry I titled “Do Jason and I want the same future?” and honestly, I still reread parts of it when I feel wobbly.

I’m not bringing this up to re-fight that night. I actually get it more now. Between my own stuff – sending Aunt Kim $250–$300 every month since February 2022 to help pay down that TD Canada Trust line of credit for Bà Ngoại’s long-term care and funeral, plus trying to save something for my RRSP – I feel the squeeze too. Rent, gas to drive up to see your folks sometimes, groceries, all of it adds up.

On top of that, work hasn’t exactly been light. Since late 2022 my low back and right shoulder have been acting up from all the transfers. When I saw Mark Jensen at Southland Physiotherapy & Wellness Centre in January 2024 and he wrote “early signs of overuse injury in OTA duties” and told me to limit heavy lifts, it scared me a bit. It made me realize I can’t just muscle through everything forever.

So when I think about moving in, I’m not picturing some Instagram-perfect condo. I’m picturing two tired people trying to protect their bodies and their parents and still have a little space to breathe. I’d rather we be honest about what we can afford and what emotional bandwidth we actually have.

Can we set aside an evening next week – maybe Thursday after dinner – to talk calmly about timelines and options? Even if the answer is “not this year,” I’d like that to be a shared decision instead of something hanging over us.

I love you, even when money and logistics are messy.

Amy

Title: Debrief after tough week – clinic + seniors
Date: 2024-06-09

I need to get this out of my head so I can sleep.

1. Work
This week at Foothills reminded me of June 2023, when Karen gave me that written warning – “Documentation lapse – reminder of charting policy” – because I forgot to finish one progress note before I left. I still feel my stomach drop when I think about it. It’s made me obsessive about double-checking every chart, which is good, but also exhausting.

The caseload is heavier again, and my shoulder and low back are grumbling. Ever since late 2022 they’ve been touchy from all the transfers. Mark Jensen at Southland Physiotherapy & Wellness Centre flagged “early signs of overuse injury in OTA duties” back in my January 2024 assessment and told me to limit heavy lifts. I’m trying, but it’s hard to say no when someone needs help.

2. Volunteering / long-term care memories
Spending time with the seniors this week stirred up memories of Bà at Silver Meadows in Edmonton. I keep flashing back to October 2021, walking into Room 214 and finding her in soiled clothing after who knows how long. We ended up writing that long complaint email, “Concerns about dignity and care for resident Huong Le – Room 214,” to the unit manager and AHS Patient Relations. No wonder I get so tense if I see anyone left waiting too long for help now.

3. Counselling
I’m grateful I’ve been seeing Dr. Melissa Carter at Calgary Counselling Centre since March 2023. The session she titled “Processing long-term care losses – May 12, 2024” felt like a turning point, but weeks like this show it’s still raw. I need to email her (subject line idea: “Struggling after tough week at the clinic and with seniors”) and maybe see if we can add an extra session.

Action items for myself:
- Ask Karen about redistributing the heaviest transfers.
- Book a follow-up with Mark if pain keeps flaring.
- Email Dr. Carter tomorrow.
- Schedule something light this weekend (Glenmore walk, Heritage Park with Jason) so life isn’t all care, all the time.

metadata.title: Debrief after tough week – clinic + seniors

artifacts.content:
Event: Counselling – Dr. Melissa Carter
Date: 2024-05-12 (Sunday)
Time: 3:00–4:00 p.m.
Location: Calgary Counselling Centre, 10th Ave SW – Office 402 (Dr. Carter)

Notes:
- This is the ongoing twice-a-month session I’ve had with Dr. Melissa Carter since March 2023 for grief/anxiety around Bà Ngoại and burnout from working with older adults.
- Today’s focus: “Processing long-term care losses – May 12, 2024” (her suggested title in the portal actually fits).

Things to bring up:
1. Silver Meadows incident
   - Revisit October 2021 at Silver Meadows Continuing Care Centre in Edmonton.
   - Walking into Room 214 and finding Bà in soiled clothes after several hours.
   - How writing that complaint email, “Concerns about dignity and care for resident Huong Le – Room 214,” to the unit manager and AHS Patient Relations still impacts how I see staff/volunteers now.

2. Work strain + body
   - Ongoing low back and right shoulder pain since late 2022 from client transfers at Foothills.
   - January 2024 assessment with physiotherapist Mark Jensen at Southland Physiotherapy & Wellness Centre – his note about “early signs of overuse injury in OTA duties” and advice to limit heavy lifts.
   - Fear of letting coworkers down if I say no.

3. Guilt + money
   - Monthly Interac e-Transfers ($250–$300) to Aunt Kim in Edmonton since Feb 2022 to help with the TD Canada Trust family line of credit for Bà’s long-term care and funeral.
   - Feeling torn between saving for my own future (RRSP, maybe a degree at U of C) and paying down that debt.

4. Boundaries with volunteering
   - How to keep showing up for seniors in the community without recreating the emotional overload of my SAIT gerontology practicum.

Reminder: After session, walk by the river or read at a café before going home. No errands.

Hi Aunt Kim,

I just finished packing some bún chả leftovers for lunch tomorrow and thought I’d send a quick update.

First, I sent this month’s transfer a bit early – you should see an Interac e-Transfer for $275 in your TD account tonight. Since February 2022 I’ve been trying to keep it between $250 and $300 each month to chip away at that TD Canada Trust family line of credit for Bà Ngoại’s long-term care and the funeral costs. Dad said at Sunday lunch that the balance is finally starting to look less scary, which is a relief.

Work at Foothills is busy but mostly good. I’m still careful with my back and right shoulder – they’ve been on and off painful since late 2022. When I saw physiotherapist Mark Jensen at Southland Physiotherapy & Wellness Centre in January 2024, he wrote “early signs of overuse injury in OTA duties” in his assessment and told me to limit heavy lifts. I’m listening (mostly!) and asking for help with the heavier transfers.

Emotionally, I’ve been thinking about Bà a lot again. I had a counselling session with Dr. Melissa Carter at Calgary Counselling Centre last month, and we spent most of it talking about those seven years in care and that awful October 2021 afternoon at Silver Meadows in Edmonton – Room 214, when we found her in soiled clothing and ended up sending the complaint email (“Concerns about dignity and care for resident Huong Le – Room 214”) to the unit manager and Alberta Health Services Patient Relations. Even now, when I’m volunteering with seniors, I notice I’m hyper-alert about whether people look comfortable and clean.

On a lighter note, Mom made canh chua this weekend and tried to claim she invented your trick of adding extra pineapple. We all know where she got it. She sent me home with three containers, so I’m basically living off your recipes.

Please let me know if the transfer doesn’t go through for any reason. Give my love to everyone in Edmonton.

Thương,
Amy

metadata.subject: This month’s transfer + a quick update

artifacts.content:
Title: Why dementia work still scares me (and why I’m drawn to it)
Date: 2024-03-18

I keep hovering over opportunities to help with dementia-specific recreation programs and then backing away. Time to be honest with myself.

Part of it goes back to SAIT in 2019. During my final semester gerontology and dementia care practicum at Bethany Riverview, I actually failed my first evaluation. Elena Morris, my supervising therapist, wrote that I seemed emotionally overwhelmed and tearful after a resident with advanced dementia became aggressive. I had to redo the whole placement in Summer 2019 under closer supervision. It was humiliating at the time, but also… accurate. I wasn’t ready.

Then there’s Bà. The October 2021 incident at Silver Meadows Continuing Care Centre in Edmonton is seared into me – walking into Room 214, seeing her left in soiled clothing for hours, the anger and shame. We wrote that detailed complaint email (“Concerns about dignity and care for resident Huong Le – Room 214”) to the unit manager and Alberta Health Services Patient Relations, but the image still pops up whenever I smell certain cleaning products.

Now I’m older, working as a junior OTA at Foothills, and I’ve been seeing Dr. Melissa Carter at Calgary Counselling Centre since March 2023 to process grief and burnout. One of our sessions was literally labeled “Processing long-term care losses – May 12, 2024.” I also know my body has limits – my low back and right shoulder have been cranky since late 2022, and Mark Jensen at Southland Physiotherapy & Wellness Centre warned in January 2024 about “early signs of overuse injury in OTA duties” and told me to be careful with heavy transfers.

So when I picture helping in a memory-care unit – music, reminiscence, gentle sensory activities – I feel both pulled and scared. I want to be the calm, consistent person I wished Bà had. I also don’t want to end up sobbing in a supply closet or wrecking my back.

Maybe the answer isn’t “never,” but “slowly, with support” – shorter shifts, clear boundaries, and lots of debriefing.

metadata.title: Why dementia work still scares me

artifacts.content:
Daniel: you free to talk later or still buried in code?

Amy: kind of wiped, might not have full sentences in me lol. But I wanted to check in.

Daniel: same. What’s up?

Amy: mix of things. Clinic has been heavy, plus the seniors I see on my days off. I actually emailed my counsellor earlier – subject line “Struggling after tough week at the clinic and with seniors.” Dr. Carter at Calgary Counselling Centre has been really good about helping me untangle grief vs burnout.

Daniel: glad you have her. sounds like a lot.

Amy: yeah. And my body is reminding me I’m not 18. My low back + right shoulder have been acting up again. Remember that January 2024 assessment at Southland Physiotherapy & Wellness Centre? Mark Jensen wrote “early signs of overuse injury in OTA duties” and told me to limit heavy lifts. I’ve been better, but this week I slipped into old habits.

Daniel: ugh. please don’t wreck yourself.

Amy: trying not to. On top of that, money brain is buzzing. I just sent Aunt Kim another $260 e-Transfer for the TD Canada Trust line of credit from Bà’s long-term care + funeral. Since Feb 2022 it’s been $250–$300 every month. It’s fine, but it does mean I’m saying no to random trips.

Daniel: I appreciate you doing that. I know Dad does too.

Amy: we’re all doing what we can. Speaking of doing what we can… how are YOU? How’s things with “Vancouver roommate stuff” these days?

Daniel: haha code phrase. it’s ok. still not ready to tell Mom + Dad I’m gay, but my friends here are solid. I’ll get there.

Amy: whenever you’re ready. No deadline. I just want you safe and supported.

Daniel: same for you. maybe we both need a vacation that costs $0.

Amy: Glenmore walk + FaceTime this weekend? Cheap and therapeutic.

Daniel: deal.

Hi Kim,

I hope this email finds you well! I wanted to share some exciting news and get your thoughts. I've decided to start volunteering at Carewest, aiming to support older adults in our community. This decision is deeply influenced by the positive impact I saw in Bà Ngoại's life during her time in long-term care, and I hope to bring that same sense of dignity and companionship to others.

I'll be volunteering weekly, likely on Mondays, Wednesdays, or Fridays, both in the mornings and afternoons for about three hours per shift. My goal is to offer consistent support, particularly in areas like rehab and recovery or community programs. I’d love to focus on dementia-specific programs if available, as I have some experience with therapeutic recreation activities from my job at the clinic. I remember how much Bà Ngoại enjoyed music and stories from her past, and I hope to bring some of that joy to others.

I'm excited to use my skills in music and crafts—maybe even lead a reading circle or a sing-along session. I’ve always believed in the power of music and storytelling to connect with people, especially seniors. Plus, having completed a Mental Health First Aid course and a workshop on dementia-friendly communication, I feel ready to be a calm and compassionate presence, adapting to each resident's unique needs.

It’s comforting to know I live just a 10-minute drive from several Carewest sites, which makes this commitment sustainable long-term. I’m not just looking for a one-time engagement; I want to build a stable routine where residents can count on me. The opportunity to balance my technical day job with something more relational is truly appealing.

In other news, Jason and I are slowly planning the logistics of moving in together, which is quite exciting. We’re considering places closer to my parents in Haysboro, which will also make visiting them easier.

Let me know if you have any advice or if you'd like to catch up over coffee soon. I’d love to hear about what’s new in Edmonton!

Take care,
Amelia
